data.title: Palantir's Moat Isn't Code: That's Why Anthropic Can't Replicate It
data.description:
Palantir Technologies, Inc. (PLTR) recently fell victim to the “SaaSpocalypse” narrative. Last week, the company’s stock saw a sharp decline on fears that the business model could be disrupted by the latest AI models of Anthropic (ANTHRO).

Palantir is one of my most successful single stock picks and still represents a sizable part of my investment portfolio. With an undeniably rich valuation, today I want to spend some time analyzing exactly how Palantir’s business could be disrupted by AI models and whether this is a realistic scenario at all.

What does Palantir do?

Contrary to popular belief, what Palantir does can be summarized in simple terms. Palantir develops highly tailored software, at scale. This software generates unique insights leading to actions that directly affect the top and bottom line of businesses.

What Palantir does could have fundamentally been done even before AI (and, in fact, the company did it, having been founded in 2003). The difference is that it could not have been done at scale but rather only by relying on hiring a number of specialists and consultants that would scale linearly with demand. Modern AI models unlocked the possibility for Palantir to scale their business, both in terms of reach and in terms of impact.

How does Palantir work? AI Ontology 101

The “how” behind the “what” Palantir does is a bit more complex. Palantir is, in my view, one of the few companies that has cracked AI. For the sake of this article, I will try explaining how Palantir works with their B2B customers in simple terms, as follows:

  • Foundry ingests and unifies customer's data from disparate silos (e.g., ERP systems, operational databases, unstructured documents) into a single, cleansed database.

  • The ontology acts as a digital twin of the enterprise, mapping real-world business objects (e.g., a "supplier," or a "production order"), their properties, and relationships. This is deeply customized per customer, not a generic template.

  • LLMs like ChatGPT are wired to the ontology, not to raw data. This means the AI reasons within defined, governed boundaries using context-rich objects rather than scanning the entire enterprise blindly.

  • AIP translates AI reasoning into action, following a Data; Logic; Action framework. The model reads from the ontology, applies business logic, and triggers outputs. This involves simple recommendations as well as fully autonomous agentic workflows.

An important concept behind the “how” of Palantir is that LLMs are, in this framework, commodities: Palantir can integrate whatever LLM best fits their solutions, and they have no need to develop their own models (one of the reasons why the company has barely any CapEx and can grow so profitably).

If readers want to dive deeper into the how behind the what, I suggest reading my previous coverage on Palantir by clicking on the links above.

Not convinced? Ask customers and look at the financials

Palantir’s skeptics have pointed out for a long time that the company is “just a consultancy” and that what they do amounts to building dashboards. I am not here to convince anyone, but I invite skeptics to consider two factors:

  1. The company has proven their solutions can impact customers’ top and bottom line.

  2. It has also proven their business can scale to a far higher degree than any traditional consultancy.

As proof of the first element, I invite readers to look at the nine AIPCon events the company has organized in the past few years. Dozens of customers have spoken directly about how Palantir’s solutions directly impact their top and bottom lines.

As proof of the second element, I invite readers to look at Palantir’s “Rule of 40” (i.e. a measure outlining how quickly and how profitably a company is growing), as pictured below.

While Palantir scales both their revenue and margins, boasting a rule of 40 of 127, traditional consultancies are undergoing a period of deep crisis and restructuring. For reference, Accenture plc (ACN), is currently growing at a Rule of 40 of ~25, using EBITDA and FCF metrics (Seeking Alpha data).

Can AI models replace Palantir?

The narrative behind Palantir’s recent correction is also fairly simple to understand and tied to the concept of a “SaaSpocalypse.” The idea is that as AI models become increasingly sophisticated, they will be able to create highly customized software at scale, without the need for software companies.

For Palantir, this means that a future AI model from Anthropic could independently build solutions that replicate Palantir’s playbook: organizing data, building an ontology, and translating everything into insights. Theoretically, this is possible.

However, as I outlined in my past coverage about the SaaSpocalypse, I think this is extremely unlikely. And, especially in the case of Anthropic, it is borderline impossible, at least based on what I've seen so far.

AI can replicate Palantir’s software, not its moat

The main reason I refute the idea of the “SaaSpocalypse” impacting Palantir is tied to the fact that software companies have a moat that AI can hardly replicate.

Even before AI, it was possible to replicate code relatively easily and build an app or a minimum viable product with a few tens of thousands of dollars, relying on freelancers or agencies. AI only brought marginal cost improvements, bringing the cost down to the hundreds of dollars.

The mistake is thinking that such an improvement will put the moats of all Software houses at risk. This is completely false in my view, as customer acquisition costs and switching costs are not impacted by AI.

The real challenge of creating a competitor to Palantir is not recreating AIP but rather scaling it by acquiring customers, and/or convincing businesses (or management) to switch to an unproven solution. No competitor, start-up, or IT department can replace Palantir’s moat with AI, in my view.

Switching costs are existential, not merely financial

Once a customer of Palantir has spent months mapping their entire operational reality into a Palantir ontology, that ontology becomes their business logic. Ripping it out is not a software migration but rather equivalent to dismantling the nervous system of the organization.

Palantir also deploys FDEs (forward deployed engineers) directly inside client operations. This embeds Palantir's thinking into the customer's culture and workflows, making the platform progressively harder to detach from.

While Palantir was among the first companies to introduce the very concept of FDE around 2010, this playbook is now being copied by other companies, including AI houses like Anthropic.

The government moat is nearly impossible to replicate

So far I have discussed Palantir’s business model only in relation to its corporate customers. When it comes to government contracts, however, the company’s moat is even stronger.

Security clearances, multi-decade institutional trust, and classified deployments create a wall that no competitor can vault over quickly. The US government alone represents ~45% of revenues and generates near-zero churn as of the latest quarter.

Why Anthropic’s $30 billion ARR is not eating Palantir’s moat

Fellow Seeking Alpha analyst Louis Gerard recently published a bearish article on Palantir, arguing that Anthropic’s reported $30 billion ARR is “eating” Palantir’s moat. I, personally, find this idea highly unlikely, for the simple reason that Anthropic operates in a completely different manner than Palantir.

Anthropic's "moat" can be disrupted by the next LLM

Anthropic's customers are building on top of Claude, the company's bespoke AI model. Palantir's customers are building inside Palantir. That distinction is crucial because it makes Palantir's NRR and retention metrics structurally superior, while Anthropic's revenue can theoretically evaporate faster if a better model appears tomorrow.

Switching costs from one LLM to another are very low, and Anthropic’s only moat is the fact that at this point in time they have one of the best AI models out there. If, six months down the line, OpenAI were to release a much better AI model, they would be ahead of the game again.

Palantir, contrary to Anthropic, is also model-agnostic. It can and does plug in Claude, GPT-4, Llama, or others through AIP. This means Anthropic's upside partly flows through Palantir in the enterprise stack, while the reverse is not true. Palantir is a customer of Anthropic's ecosystem, not a competitor.

Palantir is Anthropic's customer, not a competitor

Finally, the way that Anthropic generates revenue is entirely different from Palantir, as the company relies on the following revenue streams:

  • API, i.e. self-serve token-based access for developers and startups.

  • Direct enterprise contracts with $1M+ ACV deals where Claude replaces existing budget line items (agencies, research teams, software subscriptions).

  • Cloud distribution, since Claude is available on all three major clouds (AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, and Microsoft Foundry).

  • Claude Code, Claude Cowork, and M365 connector, all acting as workflow replacement tools that drive organic upsell. Claude Code in itself reportedly generates ~$2.5 billion per year in revenue.

As I outlined at the beginning of this article, these businesses have little to nothing to do with the way Palantir makes money. In fact, Palantir is likely a client of Anthropic, as it uses its API to integrate its models into their solutions.

About PLTR stock valuation: AI will advantage disruptors, not replace them

I do not believe that all software companies will equally be disrupted by AI. Rather, I think AI will accelerate the business of AI winners and push AI “losers” out of business. Disruption, in my view, will come from those companies that are adopting AI better and sooner than others. Palantir is one of these companies, and, as such, I think it deserves its very rich valuation, with a FWD P/E of above 100 at the time of writing.

The secret sauce of Palantir is being able to sell and scale AI-based software solutions without dealing with any of the CapEx needs that Hyperscalers investing billions in data centers have to deal with. In fact, AI hyperscaler and AI houses like Anthropic and Oracle Corporation (ORCL) are copying Palantir’s playbook by hiring FDEs and trying to find new ways to monetize their AI models.

My valuation model for Palantir, which is summarized in the table below) is based on a 10-years DCF and sees an upside of $250 per share for the company. My target price for PLTR remains unchanged following the recent sell-off. I keep rating the company a BUY.

I will not cover it in detail for the sake of this article, but I encourage readers to go through my previous work if they would like to know more.

Risks: Can AI disruptors ever be disrupted?

If AI advances to the point of achieving super-human intelligence, it is theoretically possible that software could be created at a near-zero cost, with a near-zero waiting time, and at a near-infinite scale. That could make a “SaaSpocalypse” scenario come to reality. But, in my view, it would also completely rewire the way our society works.

Instead of watching Netflix, users could vibe code their own streaming platform and create, on the spot, highly customized entertainment. Instead of relying on Windows or MacOS, user could develop a highly personalized OS, browser, office suite, and anything else they would ever need. The implications of such technology would go well beyond SaaS. If literally everything becomes “commoditized” due to AI, the very idea of private companies and money would change permanently.

This may sound sci-fi, and it is roughly in line with what Elon Musk defines as the “era of abundance” driven by AI. Given the deep changes such technology would bring to society, I find it pointless to argue to what extent a single stock (or sector) may be impacted by these dynamics. Nevertheless, this is something that readers should keep in mind as a risk to my bullish thesis.

Canary in the coal mine: What would actually be disrupted first?

If it ever became possible for companies to fully internalize their software needs with AI, I find it reasonable to believe that Palantir’s solutions would be the last to be automated. Companies would have a far bigger incentive to substitute the software that generates the most costs, such as, for example, that of Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) or SAP SE (SAP).

If an IT department could “vibe code” their company’s software needs with advanced AI models, I think we would see a “canary in the coalmine” event, in the form of earnings from all software companies coming in weak. That has not manifested in any capacity so far. But it is something that readers could keep in mind for the future, to assess whether a "SaaSpocalypse" scenario may ever be likely to manifest.

Conclusion: Palantir is an asset-light AI "Hyperscaler"

I think the entire idea behind the "SaaSpocalypse", i.e. that companies will start vibe coding their own software (including Palantir’s advanced and highly customized software), is ludicrous. Switching costs and risks make this scenario extremely unlikely, in my view.

Ultimately, companies are focused on their core business - whether that is selling packaged goods, industrial solutions, insurance, or anything in between. Businesses are not going to reinvent themselves as software houses to save the money they spend on licensing Microsoft Windows or Palantir’s AIP.

This said, I do find the “SaaSpocalypse” narrative fascinating, and I think there is some truth in it. If super-human AI ever becomes available, a complete commoditization of all non-physical businesses could become reality. This is, however, a borderline sci-fi scenario that would completely disrupt our society and economic system. I find that taking investment decisions based on such a stretched scenario makes no sense.

Yes, Palantir is expensive. But the valuation is justified when factoring in that this is one of the few (if not the only) companies that can scale AI software solutions without having to spend $1 on buying computing power or building chips or data centers. This is unlike Anthropic and other similar software houses, which are copying the playbook of Palantir in hopes of achieving profitability.

That’s why I reiterate my "Buy" for Palantir, confirming my last price target of $250 per share.

data.title: Oakmark International Small Cap Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
First Quarter 2026

  • The Fund (Investor Class) underperformed the benchmark, the MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Index (net), for the quarter.
  • At the sector level, consumer staples was the only contributor to performance, while industrials and consumer discretionary were the largest detractors from performance.
  • Geographically, the top three regional allocations are 60.7% in Europe ex U.K., 10.8% in Japan and 9.7% in the U.K. For the quarter, Mexico, South Korea and Denmark were top contributors to performance. The U.K., Italy and Sweden were top detractors from performance. Emerging markets accounted for 13.5% of the portfolio.
  • Amid heightened volatility, geopolitical headlines, and extreme stock dispersion, we believe equity markets are increasingly driven by short-term noise and crowd behavior rather than underlying business value. Accordingly, portfolios are positioned with patience and discipline, emphasizing companies trading at meaningful discounts to our estimate of intrinsic value, where long-term fundamentals—not headlines—drive expected returns.

Top Contributor | Detractor

Highlights

Top contributors

  • Jenoptik (JNPKF)
  • BNK Financial Group
  • Megacable Holdings (MHSDF)

Top detractors

  • Dometic Group (DTCGF)
  • Amplifon (AMFPF)
  • TeamViewer (TMVWF)

New purchases

  • DTS (DTSOF)
  • Minor International (MNILY)

Final sales

  • Mitie Group (MITFF)
  • Nexi (NEXPF)
  • PageGroup (MPGPF)
  • Strauss Group (SGLJF)

Top contributor

Jenoptik was the top contributor during the quarter. The Germany-headquartered optics company's stock price rose after it delivered better-than-expected results in its key Semiconductors division and indicated that demand is expected to improve in its high margin lithography business now that destocking is complete. Furthermore, performance in other divisions was strong and supportive of our view that an improved end-to-end operating model would lead to better execution and profitability. These developments were consistent with our investment thesis and support a favorable view of Jenoptik's prospects.

Top detractor

Dometic Group was the top detractor during the quarter. The Sweden-headquartered supplier of products to the mobile and outdoor living industry's stock price declined after releasing fourth-quarter earnings that fell short of consensus expectations. Later in the quarter, Dometic withdrew its dividend proposal due to rising economic uncertainty and geopolitical risk following the beginning of the Iran war. We met with management and continue to believe the stock is extremely cheap and the decision to withdraw the dividend for now is a prudent one. We appreciate their desire and ability to drive operational and strategic improvement in very difficult market conditions, which will be key to navigating a challenging environment and unlocking further value.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

New purchases

DTS is a leading systems integrator in Japan, helping companies modernize IT systems from manual, on-premise environments to automated, digital, and cloud-based platforms. The company has a long operating history and deep relationships with large enterprise customers, particularly in financial services, telecom, and manufacturing. Japanese system integrators (SI) benefit from powerful structural tailwinds as the country remains well behind other developed markets in digitization, driving a multi-year increase in IT investment. A structural shortage of IT engineers and a strong preference for onshore talent further support favorable industry dynamics. Despite these fundamentals, DTS trades at a meaningful discount to our estimate of its intrinsic value due to short-term earnings sentiment and elevated investment spending. As the company nears the end of its investment cycle, we expect earnings to accelerate and valuation to re-rate with improving operating leverage.

Minor International is a major global hospitality, restaurant, and lifestyle company. Minor operates a robust, ever-growing portfolio of over 500 hotels and 2700 restaurants spanning 68 countries. In our view, management has done a sound job of creating value through strategic acquisitions, notably the Spain-based NH Hotel Group, which we believe was essential to rounding out Minor's brand portfolio and helping it pursue an asset-light strategy. Still, investors are concerned about the company's high leverage due to its traditionally asset-heavy, owner-operator hotel model, which has led it to trade at multiples that were last seen during COVID and the Great Financial Crisis. This has allowed us to initiate a position in an industry leader that we believe will see its multiple improve as its strong free-cash-flow allows the company to de-lever and ultimately accelerate earnings growth.

data.title: FCPI: Fidelity's Disciplined Inflation-Friendly ETF Continues To Succeed
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Fidelity Stocks For Inflation ETF (FCPI) continues to perform well this year without taking any significant sector risks, and based on its strong factor mix, I expect that strong performance to continue throughout 2026. As I'll highlight below, FCPI has terrific portfolio-level earnings growth rates, trades at just 15.80x forward earnings, and has operating margins that exceed its Russell 1000 Index benchmark. FCPI's success is an excellent example of how to use fundamental analysis to find opportunities in the ETF space, so just like one year ago, I've assigned FCPI a "buy" rating, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

FCPI Overview: A Multi-Factor Strategy With Inflation-Friendly Sector Tilts

FCPI tracks the Fidelity Stocks For Inflation Factor Index, which uses a multi-factor model to select from a universe of the 1,000 largest U.S. stocks based on market cap. The Index's model calculates scores covering the value, quality, and momentum factors, equal weights them to create a composite score, adds a size factor adjustment to allow smaller stocks achieve a higher weight, and then applies adjustments to select sectors and industries based on their expected strength in rising inflation environments. A summarize of this framework is below, courtesy of the Index's methodology document.

As shown, the Index increases exposure to Energy, Materials, Consumer Staples, and Health Care with an overweight adjustment of +5% relative to the Base Index. Real Assets & Infrastructure are also overweighted at the expense of Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary, Financials, Industrials, and Technology.

It's important not to ignore the Index's factor screens. In my view, the system is pretty comprehensive, covering metrics like free cash flow yield, return on invested capital, and twelve-month earnings surprises. Each factor is weighted equally in the composite scoring system, and companies are scored compared to others in same sector.

Bank stocks are scored under a separate system, which I prefer, given how several of the metrics above (e.g., free cash flow yield) are irrelevant. ETFs like the Pacer US Cash Cows 100 ETF (COWZ) and the VictoryShares Free Cash Flow ETF (VFLO) exclude them outright, but FCPI doesn't, and instead, uses tangible book value to price, next-year earnings to price, return on equity, and debt to assets to measure a bank stock's value and quality.

Lastly, the Index selects 100 stocks at each of its semi-annual reconstitutions in February and August. FCPI has a reasonably cheap 0.15% expense ratio, but its $267 million in assets under management is pretty low for a fund with a 6.5-year track record. My hypothesis is that its name boxes it into a corner, as readers who aren't familiar with its methodology might ignore it in low-inflation environments. However, the calendar-year returns table below shows that it's been pretty competitive with the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB). 2020 was an exception, but for obvious reasons, that was a once-in-a-lifetime year, and otherwise, FCPI has done well in both bull and bear markets.

FCPI Sector Allocation Analysis vs. Peer Group

The next table highlights FCPI's sector allocations. Included in the table is IWB and four competing inflation-based funds: the Avantis Inflation Focused Equity ETF (AVIE), the First Trust Bloomberg Inflation Sensitive Equity ETF (FTIF), and the ProShares Equities For Rising Rates ETF (EQRR), and the Fidelity Dividend ETF For Rising Rates (FDRR). One key takeaway is that FCPI's sector allocations are roughly similar to IWB's, which is by design, given the +/- 5% adjustments. In contrast, AVIE, FTIF, and EQRR are highly concentrated in Energy, while FDRR's pretty balanced - not surprising, given how its Index also limits sector adjustments to +/- 5% from its benchmark universe.

High allocations to volatile sectors like Energy can be dangerous. Using EQRR as an example due to its long track record, we see below that its "best six months" return of 63.77% trounced FCPI's best return of 26.86%. However, its -31.51% "worst six months" return was about 15% worse than FCPI, and when you take it all together, FCPI easily comes out ahead. Since December 2019, its outperformed EQRR on annualized returns (13.79% vs. 11.06%), standard deviation (17.25% vs. 26.13%), and the Sortino Ratio (0.99 vs. 0.47), a key measure of downside risk-adjusted returns.

This is a good example of how FCPI's built-in limitations on active sector risk can actually protect you from overconfidence. Obviously, Energy is the hot play right now, but just like in 2022-2023, you have to know when to sell. FCPI takes some of that timing risk off the table, given its moderate ~11% exposure to the sector, while ensuring a fundamentally sound portfolio tilted toward the quality, value, and momentum factors.

FCPI Fundamental Analysis

On that note, please consider the following table that highlights selected fundamental statistics for FCPI's top 20 holdings, which comprise 58.22% of the fund. This concentration level is slightly elevated, but for an ETF that I expect will only be used as a satellite holding, it's still sufficient, in my opinion.

First, I'd like to discuss why FCPI is clearly stronger fundamentally than IWB. The table above covers all the major factors, so starting from the left, we see that its 1.00 five-year portfolio beta is less than IWB's 1.13, which indicates less market risk. Higher risk stocks like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) have less influence, but their allocations are still meaningful enough to ensure good participation in bull markets. Next, we see FCPI's 3Y EPS CAGR and one-year estimated EPS growth rates of 16.63% and 20.36% are a couple of percentage points higher than IWB's, but most importantly, its P/E ratios are much lower. In particular, I think its 19.21x trailing P/E ratio, which represents a 27% discount to IWB, will matter a lot in a market slowdown, and in this case, it is sourced mainly through the stocks that FCPI doesn't include, namely:

  • Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN): 32.59x P/E (3.59% weight in IWB)
  • Tesla, Inc. (TSLA): 320.02x P/E (1.56% weight in IWB)
  • Eli Lilly and Company (LLY): 41.62x P/E (1.23% weight in IWB)

Moving along, FCPI's 1.54% Index Yield is 0.34% higher than IWB's, which is another indication that it's tilted towards value. Its 35.48% EBIT margins and 20.19% weighted return on total capital demonstrate excellent quality and capital efficiency, and lastly, FCPI has extremely strong earnings and price momentum. Recall from the overview how its Index specifically screens for these, so it's no surprise to see its components delivered a 10.17% weighted average EPS surprise last quarter and are up 71.99% over the last year alone. To me, this is exactly what should you expect from a multi-factor model, and I believe its semi-annual reconstitution schedule is frequent enough to ensure strong factor exposure throughout the entire year.

How Does FCPI Compare With The Other Inflation-Friendly ETFs?

When I compare FCPI's factor mix to AVIE, FTIF, and EQRR, it easily wins on value, quality, and momentum, which is no surprise because they're in the factor model. FCPI's growth rates are also the best, though that may be temporary, given how it's likely linked to its momentum screens. However, in a true broadening of the market, where mega-caps no longer dominate, I can see it lagging behind. AVIE, FTIF, and EQRR have substantially lower weighted average market caps of $243 billion, $55 billion, and $92 billion, and they also have lower portfolio betas and higher gross dividend yield, which might even assist in a rotation to value or a market downturn.

Even so, all three are high risk plays because they have 40%+ allocated to Energy, by far the most volatile sector. To demonstrate why, please consider the Coefficient of Variation chart below. The Coefficient of Variation measures the standard deviation of each sector's quarterly EPS divided by the average EPS, and over the long-term (last 40 quarters), Energy's 108.6% CV figure is easily the highest, followed by Technology (42.5%) and Financials (37.1%).

In simple terms, this means AVIE, FTIF, and EQRR carry enormous earnings risk, so while they have the potential to outperform, they can also deliver big losses. We've seen that, too. As the returns distribution chart below shows, AVIE, FTIF, and EQRR had single-month losses above 5% around 8-11% of the time since April 2023 compared to only 2.7% for FCPI.

Limiting losses has paid off for both Fidelity ETFs. Since April 2023, FCPI and FDRR are up an annualized 19.42% and 17.20%, respectively, and their worst six month losses were just -1.79% and -4.10%. For me, this reinforces my long-held view that Energy is just too unpredictable even for the experts in the industry. Therefore, the prudent thing to do is to limit exposure, which FCPI does by design through its +/- 5% sector adjustments.

Investment Recommendation

In my review last year of FCPI, I stated that it wasn't as one-dimensional as you might think. While it may box itself into a corner by implying that it's designed only for high-inflation environments, FCPI is actually an excellent multi-factor ETF in disguise, offering investors 20% next-year estimated EPS growth, a 27% trailing P/E discount to IWB, and clear advantages on quality, earnings momentum, and price momentum. Importantly, it limits sector adjustments to +/- 5%, and thus, avoids going all-in on the unpredictable Energy sector. This discipline protects you from overconfidence bias, which certainly applies today, given the uncertainty regarding oil prices and the Iran War. Therefore, I continue to like it and recommend FCPI, so I've decided to reiterate my "buy" rating. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: Kolibri Global Energy: Size Matters Not In This Growth Story
data.description:
Thesis

The title of this article was clearly a play on a famous Star Wars quote by Jedi Master Yoda. The small but mighty warrior didn’t let his small stature stop him from achieving greatness.

So how exactly does this relate to investing? Small companies are able to achieve unparalleled growth rates due to the small size of their existing operations. A bolt-on asset can result in significant growth on a percentage basis. In this case, we have Kolibri Global Energy Inc. (KGEI), who is likely the smallest oil and gas producer I have covered here on Seeking Alpha.

The company operates in high oil content windows of the Anadarko region. The company targets shallower Caney intervals after selling the rights to the Woodford shale (a primary target in the region) to Exxon Mobil (XOM) several years ago.

The Caney has the luxury of having significantly higher oil cuts than traditional Anadarko plays. However, the interval displays significant material variability and high clay contents that complicate completion designs.

This creates a higher risk but higher reward situation with a producer that can easily increase production by double digits by adding one or two wells to its capital plan. The company has comfortably managed debt while building a multi-decade inventory. These characteristics are supportive of a Strong Buy rating.

Who Is Kolibri Global Energy?

KGEI is a microcap oil and gas producer that focuses on specific intervals of the Anadarko basin. Several years ago, the company sold its rights to the Woodford shale, the primary target of the day, to Exxon Mobil for $147m. The company kept the rights to what was then considered secondary targets in the Caney and Sycamore formations.

These formations are shallower reservoir rock that has absorbed migrated oil from the source rock in the Woodford interval. The lower temperatures of these formations are ideal for oil, driving up the oil content compared to the Woodford shale. KGEI’s production targets an oil window (the Tishomingo Field) that supports an oil cut in excess of 70%, which is among the best in the lower 48.

The drawback here is that the Caney formation has a high clay content, making completion jobs more complicated. The company is moderately experienced in navigating these complications with 45 Caney wells already on production.

As I mentioned in my opening remarks, KGEI is likely the smallest oil company I have covered to date here on Seeking Alpha. The company reported production levels at just over 4,000 BOE/d. To quantify how small this production level truly is, the company projects that drilling just three wells in 2026 are sufficient to grow total production by over 10%.

Originally, the company planned to drill these three wells in June. However, recent events in the Middle East have accelerated that timetable.

On April 13th, the company updated its capital plans by pulling drilling activities forward. Drilling is now expected to begin next week in an effort to capture structurally higher oil prices. This move will also ensure that 2026 results in material y-o-y production growth.

As shown below, this provides another successful data point for KGEI. The company has not grown aggressively by any means. However, the style here appears to be slow, steady, and tactical. This approach has allowed the company to gradually improve operating income despite a multi-year decline in oil prices.

Financial Outlook

Despite the accelerated drilling schedule, the company elected to maintain 2026 guidance unchanged. The company still projects to deliver 4,600 BOE/d (a 15% increase) and generate $57.5m in annual EBITDA, assuming $74/barrel WTI pricing (CL1:COM). This guidance is unchanged from mid-March when drilling was scheduled to commence in June.

Under the original drilling plan, the three new wells would have likely only provide a few months of production in calendar 2026. Thus, an additional two months of production could result in a meaningful uplift in total production. This sets up a scenario where KGEI is likely to outperform the provided guidance.

Additionally, by pulling drilling rigs forward, the company holds the potential to keep these rigs active in the second half of the year should commodity prices warrant additional investment. As shown below, average drilling times in 2024 were just under two weeks. This leaves significant calendar white space to drive production levels in 2026.

The odds of keeping rigs active for longer is becoming more and more likely as the Middle East crisis continues. The record high oil prices are extremely beneficial for KGEI’s high oil cut. The combined opportunity for higher margin and higher production levels positions the company as a solid bet to exceed guidance targets.

Valuation

To value KGEI, I took two separate approaches. The first is the standard EV:EBITDA metrics using 2026 guidance. The second will review the net present value of KGEI’s reserves against the company’s enterprise value. In both cases we see a favorable outcome.

From a standard approach, KGEI trades at roughly 3.5x projected 2026 EBITDA. As previously noted, this is likely to be conservative based on the current oil strip versus the $74/barrel assumption used in guidance.

From a long-term perspective, KGEI holds significant resource potential for the value of its shares. Based on proved reserves, the company holds over 20 years of inventory at current production levels. This figure expands to 30 years if probable reserves are credited into the inventory base.

This massive inventory level translates into a significant net present value benefit. As shown below, the company estimates that the discounted value of its reserves amounts to nearly $700m of proven plus probable reserves.

The current enterprise value of KGEI is approximately $210m, making for a severe undervaluation compared to the company’s owned inventory (remember net present value is also a discounted methodology). Further, this current undervaluation does not reflect the potential upside that is offered by the stacked play of the Anadarko basin.

The company has unbooked reserves in the Sycamore and T-zone formations. With plenty of work to keep this small company occupied in the Caney formation, the need to fully explore these intervals likely hasn’t manifested yet.

To this point, the company believes the Caney interval extends into it’s eastern acreage. This is yet another opportunity for testing and exploration to deepen the company’s asset base.

Risk Versus Reward

KGEI is quite small and is valued quite attractively. At these production levels, drilling a few wells can result in significant growth and cash flows. Current guidance (with conservative price assumptions) indicates that the company is capable of producing a free cash flow yield of 20%. This cash is being used to buyback shares and keep the balance sheet in a healthy condition.

At current oil prices, the free cash flow yield could potentially be significantly higher. Unfortunately, the company is roughly 50% hedged with costless collars that limit some of the upside offered here. The company has stated that the upper limit on these collars is approximately $77.25/barrel.

Fortunately, the company appears to be making good use of the current crisis by securing lucrative swap contracts through June (as of the March 19th earnings call). Assuming the company has maintained this opportunistic stance, these and any additional hedges should be quite accretive to the bottom line.

The downside here is that it leaves little room for error. Every well needs to hit. A dry hole, a casing failure, or unintended parent/child well interactions could be quite disastrous to production levels. There just isn’t a sizeable asset base to fall back on.

The acceleration in production provides an obvious opportunity for growth in this small company. Is KGEI going to turn into your next large cap producer? Probably not.

The more probable outcome is that the company is sold for inventory replenishment to one of the bigger fish in the industry. In my opinion, the company needs to start investing in proving out its uncredited inventory base. Doing so either increases the price tag of the company or allows the company to repeat the sale of specific intervals like it did with Exxon Mobil over a decade ago. Executing such a plan requires talented management and opportunistic timing.

This investment may be more of a swing for the fences style opportunity. Fortunately, the investment is risk adjusted with significant inventory value and attractive operating costs. As shown below, the company has unusually low operating costs per BOE for company of this size. Time will tell if this efficiency can be sustained as the company adds more wells and explores new zones.

Investor Key Takeaways

  1. KGEI is a small producer with significant inventory. At current production levels, proven reserves extend beyond 20 years of production.
  2. Extending this figure to probable reserves extends inventory duration to beyond 30 years of high oil content production (>70%).
  3. The company has decided to accelerate investment in light of a rapid increase in oil prices. This will benefit KGEI’s high oil cut but will be slightly limited by existing hedging contracts. The company will be looking to increase the value of its hedge book during this time of elevated prices.
  4. KGEI is currently capable of generating a 20% FCF yield. This is yet another display of the value proposition offered here. The company trades at 3.5x 2026 EBITDA and roughly 30% of the net present value of the company’s inventory.
  5. I see KGEI as a potential inventory source for larger producers. The stacked play offers the opportunity to sell rights of specific intervals. The company has successfully implemented this strategy before. The company must continue to develop the acreage outside of its core operating area to make this strategy successful.
  6. I rate KGEI as a Strong Buy due to significant undervaluation, a strong oil market, and accelerating production.

data.title: Imperial Brands: Reiterating Guidance Is Not Good Enough
data.description:
Imperial Brands (IMBBY) is a company that I've covered quite a bit since I hold a substantial long-term position that was acquired at the height of the tobacco bear market a few years ago. In my latest article written in March, I downgraded the shares from a "Buy" to a "Hold" based on valuation concerns alone.

After more than 200% total returns since the bear market lows, the 13x P/E was still slightly below my estimate of intrinsic value, but the margin of safety wasn't large enough to initiate new positions, hence the downgrade.

Imperial Brands just released its H1 26 pre-close trading update. For those who are unfamiliar with British companies, it's just a short statement issued before the release of the half-year report next month.

The most important metrics to watch are revenues, and the resulting cash generation out of those sales. Imperial Brands comes with a high shareholder yield, but very little underlying growth of the business. In order to keep generating great returns, the business needs to earn enough cash to fund the buybacks and dividends.

When I saw the release this morning, my first impression was excitement. Here we have a company that just a few years ago was trading at a double-digit dividend yield and a mid-single digit P/FCF ratio, and it keeps firing on all cylinders. As the saying goes, those declared dead live longer.

The market, however, wasn't impressed. As of this writing, the Imperial Brands shares traded in London are down by over 7%. Not only that, but it seems like the trading update took down the whole tobacco sector, as British American Tobacco shares lost more than 4% on a day when most stocks are in the green. Let's quickly go through the short trading update to see what it means for investors.

Half Year 2026 Pre-Close Trading Update

The first sentence of the trading update is: We reiterate our full-year guidance for fiscal year 2026. That's not a bad outcome given that spiking oil prices tend to hurt the purchasing power of consumers and historically have been negatively correlated with cigarette volumes.

The company expects to drive low-single-digit growth in tobacco & NGP revenues in H1. Sounds good to me, considering that many consumer staples peers are struggling right now.

Quite a few companies in the food or alcohol industry are not only facing lower volumes currently, but they're reporting lower profit margins and cash flows. The tobacco industry, as expected, is chugging along nicely even in this challenging environment when many lower-income consumers are short of cash. A growing part of smokers belong to this group.

It shows the resilience of the tobacco sector. Not only are cigarettes addictive, but the pricing power remains intact even during times of high inflation and cash-strapped consumers.

We have to remember, the tobacco industry is not a high-growth industry; it's only growing in the low-single-digits. It likely will stay this way. So, Imperial Brands expecting low-single-digit revenue growth and slightly higher adjusted operating profit is in line with the industry. Reiterating full-year guidance at a time of high volatility and uncertainty is good news. But it gets better.

Imperial Brands expects to deliver at least high-single-digit adjusted EPS growth for the full year. This includes the ongoing £1.45 billion buybacks, which are the main driver of EPS growth in its recent past and will likely be the major force for returns going forward, along with the dividend. The shareholder yield sits at 12.10% and the lower the share price gets, the higher the shareholder yield rises.

After the mini-crash, shares are trading at 11.4x last year's earnings. For now, I am keeping my "Hold" rating, but if the valuation were to once again decline to the single-digits, I'll upgrade it to "Buy". Slow-growth companies have to be bought at low valuations in order to achieve great returns. That said, if your return expectations and are lower and your risk tolerance higher than mine, there's nothing wrong with accumulating at current levels.

Expected Total Returns

The dividend sits at 5.59% and the margin of safety is once again increasing. Over the medium term, I expect 4 to 5% growth in EPS due to the ongoing buybacks for a total return of ∼10% from here.

5.59% dividend + 4.5% growth in EPS = 10.09% total return

A declining share price is good news for patient long-term investors. Since most of the growth in EPS comes from the buybacks, Imperial Brands' underlying business can be best described as stagnant.

The lower the share price gets, the more shares the company will retire and the less it has to pay out in dividends, which further adds to the stability of the dividend and helps increase the growth rate. It's a pill that's hard to swallow, but as long-term investors, it's the right way to think about it.

Why Did the Share Price Decline?

Now, if there wasn't any major bad news today, why did the share price decline by more than 7% right after the release?

I've been investing for many years, but I still can't find a reason for the irrational behavior, especially on an earnings release day. Sometimes, when great results are released, the share price tanks. Other times, results come in well below my and other analysts' expectations and the share price rises. I stopped trying to make sense of it a long time ago; as investors, we have to accept that short-term price movements involve a large degree of randomness.

From April 2024 to February 2026, the shares have gained 110% in USD terms or 88% in GBP.

There were only minor corrections, and it seems like the time has come for a larger correction. All tobacco stocks are below their recent highs, it seems like the whole sector is consolidating.

If I had to find a negative in the trading update, it would be these few statements:

The conflict in the Middle East has resulted in a more uncertain geopolitical and macro environment. Whilst there has been no material business impact to date, the potential future impact during the second half remains uncertain.

We continue to monitor the situation and will give a further update with our H1 results announcement on 12 May.

There has been no material impact from the conflict in the Middle East so far, but the future is uncertain. "No material impact" is good, but the stock market hates uncertainty. Guess what, the future is always uncertain. Then there's this statement:

While market share remains important, we continue to balance share and value. Having successfully stabilised our aggregate share across our top five markets, we continue to evolve our approach to reflect changing market dynamics and our focus on more profitable segments, to deliver long-term, sustainable value creation. As such, we anticipate some overall modest aggregate share reduction across the top five markets in H1, alongside growth in tobacco adjusted operating profit.

Modest aggregate share reduction doesn't sound good, but as long as it leads to higher profits and dividends, I couldn't care less.

And finally, what might have spooked the market is the following:

...NGP adjusted operating losses are expected to be moderately higher... NGP net revenue is expected to be lower than the same period last year.

The market is extensively focused on New Generation Products (NGP). After all, that's where the future of the industry lies. But in the case of Imperial Brands, I believe it's the wrong approach. The company likely doesn't have the resources nor the ambition to compete in NGPs in a meaningful way. In my view, Imperial Brands is a pure play on tobacco.

In 2025, NGPs were just 4.44% of their total tobacco & NGP revenues. The segment was growing at 13.7% last year and Imperial Brands is losing money in it. In my opinion, it's unlikely that they can compete against rivals with deeper pockets like British American Tobacco and Philip Morris. I'm not concerned about lower NGP revenues in the short or the long term.

Conclusion

Imperial Brands delivered a trading update that's in line with previous guidance, but sometimes that's not good enough. Short-term volatility can lead to long-term opportunities and if shares were to decline further from here, I'd once again upgrade them to a "Buy". For now, the stock remains a "Hold".

The company is operating in a low-growth industry, and in order to achieve great returns, the valuation paid is important. Cigarette volumes are falling and have done so for quite some time. The most important thing when it comes to low-growth industries with declining demand is having a wide margin of safety, and, with patience, we might get there.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: WDI: Collect High-Yield Income From This Discounted, Diversified Fixed Income Fund
data.description:
In my recent article covering 7 high-yield fixed income CEFs that are trading at a wider than average discount, one of those that I mentioned is Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI).

One of the criteria for evaluating whether a CEF is a potential buy candidate is the current discount (or premium) to NAV that the fund trades at based on the latest market price. One advantage to investing in CEFs is the ability to buy funds that are trading at a wider than average discount, because the fund managers often take steps to close that discount, or because the market adjusts the price closer to NAV after positive market sentiment drives the price higher. Either way, there is typically a price pattern that can be taken advantage of based on the trading history of a specific CEF.

Although WDI is less than five years old, it has followed a pattern similar to its peer funds like KKR Income Opportunities Fund (KIO), Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC), and PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) in the past three years delivering similar (and in fact, better) total returns than those multi-asset fixed income funds.

In this review, I want to take a more detailed look at WDI and suggest that income investors with a long-term horizon (ten to twenty years or more), might want to consider taking a position in this fund now while it trades at a much wider than average discount.

Before I get into the details on WDI, I want to reiterate my reasoning behind the recent market price action that has negatively impacted fixed income funds. Interest rates are one factor and we witnessed in 2022 how rapidly rising inflation caused interest rates to soar from near zero to over 4% in a matter of months. That negatively impacted WDI especially, which had just launched in June 2021. We can easily see on the 5-year price chart how WDI lost a lot of value in 2022 before recovering in 2024 as rates began to stabilize.

The fund’s holdings in high-yield bonds, bank loans, and CLOs make up the majority of the fund portfolio and all three of those types of fixed income securities delivered strong performance in Q4 2025 as explained in the performance review section of the fund commentary. Another factor contributing to positive Q4 performance was the securitized credit exposure, specifically the MBS securities in the fund portfolio.

  • The Fund's exposure to high-yield bonds, bank loans and CLOs was in aggregate the most significant contributor during the quarter given their high income generation.
  • The Fund's securitized credit exposure, mainly to non-agency residential and commercial mortgage-backed securities, was also a positive contributor.
  • Emerging markets exposure, particularly to USD-denominated sovereigns, contributed modestly to the Fund's performance.
  • The Fund's duration and curve positioning was a slight contributor as the UST yield curve twisted steeper, with shorter-term yields falling, while longer-term yields rose.

Although 2025 ended on a positive note, the start of 2026 has seen a lot of fear due at least in part to the private credit scare, driving down market prices of many of the fixed income funds that I have been following. WDI is no exception and saw its market price decline from what was a small premium in the summer of 2025 to near its widest discount in a year now at -5.5% (as of 4/10/26).

WDI Overview and Key Facts

WDI is a multisector bond fund with some interesting characteristics that set it apart from other similar funds. The fund description from the website spells out the details of the fund strategy and some key facts, including the termination provision. All twelve of the CEFs that were launched in 2021 had limited terms of 12 years before they either liquidate (at NAV) or solicit votes to convert to perpetual prior to the termination date.

Diverse opportunity set: Invests in a wide range of fixed income securities, seeking to go beyond traditional bond benchmarks to access a broad range of opportunities for income and capital appreciation. Income-focused: Potential access to fixed income sectors and private debt not typically available through traditional mutual funds. Flexible and dynamic: Rotates sectors and securities in response to market conditions, focusing on what we believe are undervalued securities with attractive fundamentals. Limited Term: The Fund intends to liquidate on or about June 24, 2033.

While the fund still has seven years before it faces the termination decision, that characteristic can help to drive a narrowing of the discount as the fund approaches the liquidation date. Therefore, it is something to keep in mind for long-term investors.

As of 2/28/26 according to the fund website, WDI had just over $1.1B in total assets employing about 33% leverage (in the form of loans and reverse repos) and portfolio holdings had an effective duration of just under 4 years, while the weighted average life of the holdings (days until maturity) was about 8.5 years.

The other interesting point is the flexible and dynamic nature of the portfolio holdings, which can be adjusted based on current market conditions. As an income-focused fund, there are opportunities available when market prices are driven lower due to market sentiment but fundamentals remain intact. I believe that is the case right now in mid-April 2026 as the recent impacts of the Iran war have pushed oil prices higher sending a ripple effect through global markets due to rising fears of rapidly increasing inflation, just like we witnessed in 2022.

According to the fund website, the largest concentration in the portfolio as of 3/31/26 was in High-Yield Credit at 45% followed by CLOs, CMBS, and bank loans. Non-agency MBS and a smattering of other fixed income instruments make up the remainder.

The fund sponsor, Western Asset, is one of the Franklin Templeton investment managers with over 50 years’ experience (originally founded in 1971) as an active fixed income manager. As mentioned in the fund description, the fund managers have access to fixed income securities and private debt not typically available to other mutual funds.

The top holdings shown on the fund website indicate a position size under 1% for all but the top 4, which together comprise just over 5% of the total portfolio value. The concentration risk is minimized with that level of diversification.

WDI Distribution History

Although it has a relatively short history of less than 5 years since going public, WDI has increased the monthly distribution several times and has paid out “special” distributions each year by making two distributions in the month of December.

In January of this year the payment date was changed to the end of the month so it appears on the chart that a month was skipped, however, that is not the case.

As of 4/13/26 the yield at market price was about 13% and 12.4% at NAV as shown on the fund website.

Dividend Reinvestment Plan

The fund does offer a DRIP (Dividend Reinvestment Plan) discount when shares are trading at a premium. The full text of the DRIP is spelled out in the Annual Report (see page 105) and I have included a summary of the relevant paragraphs for those interested in taking advantage of reinvested distributions, which can help to compound your future income stream even faster.

When the fund trades at a premium to NAV:

Fund will issue new Common Shares at a price equal to the greater of (A) the net asset value per share at the close of trading on the NYSE on the payment date or (B) 95% of the market price per share of the Common Shares on the payment date.

When the fund trades at a discount to NAV:

Fund will buy Common Shares in the open market, on the NYSE or elsewhere, for your account as soon as practicable commencing on the trading day following the payment date and terminating no later than the earlier of (A) 30 days after the dividend or distribution payment date, or (B) the payment date for the next succeeding dividend or distribution to be made to the shareholders.

Since its inception, the fund has rarely traded at a premium so the DRIP discount is something to keep on your radar if you do want to take advantage of it when the price returns to premium territory at some point in the future.

WDI Performance

Because of the brief relative history of the fund, a chart of calendar year returns is probably the best way to review and evaluate past performance. From the fund website, the fund underperformed in 2022 like most other fixed income and equity funds that year, but delivered strong performance in 2023 followed by very good performance in 2024 and 2025. Performance slowed during the first quarter of 2026 as the market price declined even further than the NAV in the first two months of this year.

Risks to WDI Buy Thesis

The biggest risk is that the economy suddenly lurches into a deep global recession due to the impact of much higher oil prices as it begins to trickle down to negatively affect everything from the cost of airline fuel to farm fertilizer. This is a very real possibility that the market seems to be overlooking for now, but could become reality later this year if things continue as they have been for the past few weeks with no end in sight yet for the Iran conflict.

Other risks include a potential decision by the Fed to raise interest rates due to suddenly higher inflation in the next few months, suppressing growth and causing stock market declines to spillover into fixed income sending prices lower.

One way to offset those risks is by acquiring shares of the fund when the discount is substantially lower than average. The long-term track record of peer funds like PDI, GOF, KIO, and ARDC support the thesis of buying when the discount is low (or the premium is lower than average). Generally speaking, each of those funds have recovered from past market corrections within a matter of months and have followed very similar trading patterns for the past five years.

Summary: Buy WDI While Discount is Wide and Collect 13% Yield

Although the fund does not have a long track record to analyze it has many other things going for it. The fund is managed by an experienced management team with more than 50 years in the fixed income securities management business. The portfolio mix is well diversified and optimizes current market conditions with its mix of mostly high-yield corporate bonds along with senior loans and CLOs. The income from the portfolio holdings easily supports the high-yield distribution and typically pays out an additional or special distribution each year to meet the requirements of an RIC (Regulated Investment Company).

An RIC must earn at least 90% of its income from capital gains, interest, or dividends from investments. Further, an RIC must distribute a minimum of 90% of its net investment income in the form of interest, dividends, or capital gains to its shareholders.

According to the fund’s Annual Report, offering a steady, reliable monthly distribution is the goal.

The Fund has a practice of seeking to maintain a relatively stable level of distributions to shareholders. This practice has no impact on the Fund’s investment strategy and may reduce the Fund’s NAV. The Fund’s manager believes the practice helps maintain the Fund’s competitiveness and may benefit the Fund’s market price and premium/discount to the Fund’s NAV.

For a retiree seeking reliable monthly income now yielding 13%, I suggest taking a look at WDI.

data.title: Bank of America: A Higher-For-Longer Rate Play
data.description:
Higher energy prices and a potentially changing tune of the Federal Reserve with respect to rate cuts could be game-changing events this year for Bank of America Corporation (BAC). The bank just reported better-than-expected results for its first fiscal quarter, mainly due to strong activity in its consumer-oriented business. Most importantly, the bank recorded significant gains in its net interest income -- which rose 17% year-over-year amid a higher-for-longer interest rate environment -- and managed to expand its loan portfolio.

While the consensus has been until recently that the Federal Reserve will push for a federal fund rate cuts in 2026, an increase in inflation driven by the February start of a new war in the Middle East, has the potential to fundamentally strengthen the earnings picture of Bank of America. With the Fed likely going to delay rate cuts until inflation is back under control, the Wall Street bank faces an attractive interest rate landscape that could spur further NII gains in the quarters ahead.

Previous rating

I rated Bank of America a Buy despite growing rate cut expectations at the beginning of the year -- Buy The Pullback On NII Strength -- because the Wall Street bank exhibited significant net interest income strength. In the first-quarter, Bank of America generated considerable net interest income and shifting interest rate expectations have the potential to boost the bank's earnings further in the quarters ahead. Since the consumer banking business is in excellent shape and the loan portfolio is growing, I maintain a Buy rating following the Bank of America's Q1 '26 earnings scorecard.

Bank of America crushes Q4 ’25 expectations

Bank of America published higher-than-expected earnings and revenue results for Q1 ’26, supported by strong results in the core lending business as well as in consumer banking: the bank published $1.11 per-share in GAAP earnings vs. a consensus estimate of $1.02 per-share. Bank of America also sailed past top line estimates by reporting $30.3B in total revenue for the first-quarter, beating the estimate by ~$350M.

Bank of America is benefiting from a resilient and growing U.S. economy that fundamentally supports its core lending operations: the bank published $30.3B in total revenues for the first fiscal quarter, showing 7% year-over-year growth. Further, the Wall Street bank posted net income of $8.6B, highlighting a year-over-year increase of 21%, with BofA seeing specific growth tailwinds from its consumer-facing banking business in the U.S.

Bank of America’s financial results were mainly driven by three factors: 1) Strong growth in the consumer segment, 2) Higher net interest income (the most significant component), and 3) An expansion in its loan portfolio.

As to the first point, Bank of America's consumer operations -- mainly personal loans and other banking services -- contributed $3.1B in profits to the bank's total net earnings in the first fiscal quarter (+21% Y/Y). While not the fastest growth rate for BofA (Global Wealth and Investment Management saw a 32% Y/Y profit jump), the bank's consumer business is by far the largest.

Net interest income and resurgence of inflation

The March CPI report was not a good one for consumers, but it might turn out to be a good one for Wall Street banks that rely on high interest rates for their variable rate loan portfolios.

In March, inflation reaccelerated to 3.3% -- the highest level since March 2024 -- which was driven mainly by an increase in energy prices related to the war on Iran. Petroleum prices surged to more than $100 per barrel immediately after the outbreak of the war on February 28, 2026 and since energy prices are a core component of the inflation index, higher inflation figures strongly tilt the odds in favor of delayed rate cuts for the financial sector. This, of course, represents a major catalyst for the bank's net interest income, a key component for Bank of America's net earnings.

Bank of America posted $15.7B in net interest income in the fourth-quarter (+$1.3B, +9% Y/Y), highlighting a strong lending environment for banks with large loan portfolios. The bank benefited from both higher-for-longer interest rates as well as a larger loan portfolio which was driven especially by gains made in the credit market. Positive deposit and loan trends as well as fixed-rate asset repricing drove gains for Bank of America in this regard.

In the first quarter, Bank of America grew its average loans to $1.19T, showing a massive 9% year-over-year growth rate. This growth stemmed mostly from high demand for loans in the Global Markets and Global Wealth and Investment Management businesses. Most importantly, with the market suffering the above-mentioned increase in inflation, the Federal Reserve is now much less likely to lower federal fund rates in the short term. Since Bank of America estimates that a 100 (parallel) basis point shift to the downside will impact the bank's net interest income negatively by $2.0B over a twelve-months period, delayed rate cuts immediately benefit the bank's bottom line in the next several quarters.

Bank of America’s valuation

Bank of America has seen a significant valuation rebound in March, in-line with the broader market, but shares still have upside potential in my opinion, especially in the context of higher energy prices reaccelerating inflation. Shares of Bank America are currently valued at a price-to-book ratio of 1.38X compared to 1.53X for Wells Fargo (WFC). Both banks are set to be beneficiaries of delayed rate cuts as they own a considerable amount of variable rate-paying loans on their balance sheets.

In my last review of Bank of America I calculated a fair value of $64 per-share for the Wall Street bank -- based off of historical valuation ratios -- which calculates to ~20% upside potential. Most importantly, with the market now expecting a higher-for-longer rate environment, Bank of America actually has a catalyst for NII growth that previously did not exist. Therefore, I see my $64 price target for BofA more as a low-end fair value estimate.

Risks with Bank of America

The biggest risk for Bank of America right now would be a decline in petroleum prices which would likely precipitate lower core inflation. This in turn could result in an accelerating timeline of federal fund rate cuts which would negatively impact Bank of America’s earnings outlook in the short and medium term. What would therefore get me to change my mind about Bank of America is if the lender were to see a decline in its net interest income (lower NII margins) as well as slower loan growth.

Final thoughts

Bank of America submitted a very decent earnings sheet for the first fiscal quarter on Wednesday that beat analyst expectations, and that showed continued gains on the most important metric for banks: its net interest income. In Q1 ’26, Bank of America report $8.6B in net interest income showing a 21% year-over-year increase, due to a strong interest and loan backdrop.

I like that Bank of America is expanding its loan portfolio and posting strong consumer banking results, supported by robust economic performance in the U.S. With shares trading about 17% below my (unchanged) fair value target, I believe the risk profile here is skewed to the upside, mainly because the Federal Reserve has a strong incentive to postpone NII-eroding interest rate cuts.

data.title: Davenport Core Leaders Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Q1 2026 Market Review

The Davenport Core Leaders Fund (DAVPX) declined 6.18% for the quarter, slightly lagging the S&P 500® Index, which fell 4.33%. Market conditions during the quarter were unsettled, as early period rotation away from technology and software stocks gave way to a sharp rally in the energy sector amid intensifying Middle East tensions. Sector allocation was a key driver of relative performance, with underweight positioning in Energy and Consumer Staples weighing most heavily as leadership shifted. While stock-specific underperformance impacted results, these headwinds were partially offset by favorable sector positioning in Industrials and Materials, as well as an underweight exposure to Technology and Communication Services. Despite the range of challenges encountered during the quarter, our strategy remains consistent and focused on owning high quality, competitively advantaged growth companies that we believe are well positioned to deliver attractive long term results.

Fund Update

Contributors: The Fund benefited from exposure to energy and energy related companies during the first quarter as the conflict with Iran contributed to a sharp rise in oil prices. Notable positive impacts from companies with energy-aligned businesses were seen in both EOG Resources, Inc. (EOG) and Quanta Services, Inc. (PWR).

Despite broad weakness among technology stocks, individual holdings such as Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI) and Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) contributed meaningfully to quarterly performance, reflecting solid contribution within a sector where the Fund remained significantly underweight.

Detractors: During the first quarter, ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), Accenture PLC (ACN), and Microsoft Corp (MSFT) were among the largest detractors within the Fund, as renewed pressure across the Technology sector—particularly concerns around artificial intelligence driven disruption—weighed on performance. In addition, weakness among several Health Care holdings impacted results, with both Intuitive Surgical, Inc. (ISRG) and UnitedHealth Group, Inc. (UNH) declining during the period.

Fund Activity

During the quarter, we initiated a new position in Constellation Energy Corp. (CEG). The company operates the largest nuclear fleet in the United States, with approximately 22 gigawatts of capacity, generating more than 180 million megawatt hours annually. This scale provides Constellation with meaningful competitive advantages, including high barriers to entry, industry-leading capacity factors, and growing opportunities to secure long-term, premium priced contracts as data center demand and electrification trends intensify. Beyond nuclear, Constellation's natural gas, renewables, and storage assets enhance earnings resilience, while its investment-grade balance sheet, expanding free cash flow, and disciplined capital allocation framework support long-term growth. Collectively, these attributes position Constellation as a beneficiary of structural growth in U.S. electricity demand and support our decision to initiate a new position in the stock.

We also engaged in a range of investment weighting changes as we adjusted position sizing to reflect emerging valuation opportunities and concerns during a volatile quarter. We added to positions in several technology companies including Accenture PLC (ACN), Apple, Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft Corp (MSFT), and ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW). We also trimmed investments in some technology holdings including Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI), Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META).

Conclusion

The first quarter was marked by heightened volatility and rapid shifts in market leadership, creating a particularly challenging investment backdrop. Despite these challenges, we remain focused on the long term – aligning ourselves with high quality businesses that have durable competitive advantages, strong returns on capital and management teams committed to disciplined, long term capital allocation. Although this approach may lag during periods of abrupt leadership rotation, we believe it positions the Fund to compound intrinsic value more reliably over full market cycles. Looking ahead, we remain confident that staying grounded in business fundamentals—rather than attempting to time short term market dynamics—provides the most effective path to long term risk adjusted returns across a wide range of economic and market conditions.
